does: This paper identifies and formalizes a fundamental limitation in current AI safety assurance methods. The authors demonstrate that behavioural evaluations and red-teaming—the primary tools used in existing AI governance frameworks enacted between 2019 and 2026—can only access observable model outputs and outputs, leaving them epistemically incapable of verifying the latent representations, internal objectives, and long-horizon agentic behaviors that governance frameworks now demand to regulate. They formalize this structural mismatch as the audit gap and introduce the concept of fragile assurance to describe cases where the evidence collected does not actually support the safety claims being made. Through systematic analysis of 21 assessment instruments across governance frameworks, they show how current practice creates a divergence between what regulators require as proof and what assurance methods can actually deliver.
connects: This work builds on longstanding concerns in AI safety research about the interpretability and transparency of neural networks, extending the critique into the regulatory and governance domain. It responds directly to the proliferation of AI safety frameworks and mandatory safety evaluations that have emerged as governance has accelerated, drawing on earlier work in mechanistic interpretability, adversarial robustness testing, and the limitations of behavioural approaches to understanding model internals. The paper also engages with scholarship on assurance cases and evidence hierarchies in safety-critical systems, while connecting to broader governance analysis showing how institutional pressures and incentives shape the adoption of verification methods. It builds implicitly on red-teaming literature that has explored both the strengths and gaps of behavioural evaluation as a safety tool.
enables: This position paper sets up several critical follow-up directions: developing novel verification methods that can access and verify latent representations and long-horizon behaviors beyond surface outputs, which becomes an urgent research priority if governance demands remain as currently formulated; re-examining the evidential standards and feasibility of safety claims in AI governance, potentially leading to reformed regulatory requirements that are epistemically aligned with achievable assurance methods; and investigating mechanistic approaches to verification including interpretability techniques, mechanistic probing, and structural analysis that might bridge the audit gap. It also points toward governance reform that either reduces claimed safety assurances to what is actually verifiable or invests in fundamentally new verification technologies. The paper implicitly calls for greater honesty about the limits of current assurance and the risks of fragile safety claims in high-stakes deployment decisions.
